upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, nearly isothermal surface layer with a strong inversion near 850 mb (~1500 m / ~4900 ft MSL) where temperature jumps to 14.2°C — this effectively caps thermals well below 5000 ft AGL. The lapse rate from surface to 850 mb is only -1.89°C/km, extremely stable and far below even the moist adiabatic rate, indicating a strongly suppressed thermal environment. Winds are westerly at 9 kts at the surface, backing slightly to 285°/12 kts at 850 mb, then 250°/11 kts at 700 mb — no significant shear, but the capping inversion will limit thermal development and BL heights to roughly 300–400 m AGL at most inland sites today.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KSFO showing FEW015 at 2056Z (a low marine cloud fragment) but clearing by 2156Z to SCT200 — marine layer influence minimal by late afternoon. KHAF showing FEW015 earlier in the day, now CLR, confirming marine layer largely burned off at the coast. No significant aviation hazards noted.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW-facing upper launch
sites.howItWorks: The 1750 ft launch at Ed Levin works best as a thermal site with NW–W winds at 8–18 kts to provide reliable cycles. The BL needs to develop above launch elevation (~530 m MSL) for pilots to work thermals above ridge height. Wind direction 280–320° is ideal; lighter winds produce weak, narrow thermal streets while stronger NW can create punchy ridge lift.
sites.todaySetup: The decisive factor is a shallow boundary layer capped hard by the 850 mb inversion (~1500 m MSL), which limits thermal height to only ~350 m AGL from this launch — barely enough to stay aloft without aggressive scratching. Morning is dead calm with 100% cloud cover through 8 AM, clearing quickly by 9 AM. Winds build from near-zero at 10 AM (0.4 kts, variable) to a usable 4–5 kts WNW by noon and 7–8 kts WNW by 2–3 PM. KRHV shows 22–23°C and clear skies by 2–3 PM local time, confirming thermal potential inland. However, BL tops in the models only reach 325–350 m, meaning thermals will be shallow and cycles potentially punchy as the sea breeze kicks in. Tomorrow.io data is for Apr 8, not available for today at this launch specifically — relying on HRRR/GFS which agree well here. The afternoon window 1–4 PM is the only viable period, but expect weak, low thermals with a sharp cap.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Overcast at 8 AM clearing rapidly. Winds nearly calm (0.4–2.8 kts, variable WNW). Too light and unstable directionality for reliable cycles — not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds picking up to 4–7 kts WNW (287–296°). BL reaching 340–350 m. Thermals developing but shallow and capped. Marginal but potentially flyable for experienced P3 pilots comfortable with low ceiling work.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 7–8 kts WNW (296–300°) at 2–3 PM, BL holding at ~325–350 m. Sea breeze cycles should be established. Cloud cover increasing after 5 PM (36–43%) as marine influence returns. Window closes around 5–6 PM.

verdict.hazards: Very low thermal ceiling (~350 m AGL), sharp inversion cap, variable morning winds, marine layer return after 5 PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft launch)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is primarily a soaring/sled-ride site that works with consistent NW–WNW winds in the 8–15 kt range providing ridge lift along the lower hill. It's more forgiving than the upper launch but requires steady wind direction. Thermals are less critical here — ridge effect and reliable cycles matter more.
sites.todaySetup: The lower launch has a better wind profile today than the upper, with surface winds already at 5+ kts from the NW by 8 AM and building to a solid 7–9 kts WNW through the afternoon. This is actually the more viable Ed Levin option today: the 600 ft hill will see consistent ridge lift from the developing NW sea breeze without relying on the shallow, capped thermal column. KRHV confirms clearing skies and warming to 22–23°C by early afternoon, supporting the sea breeze cycle. The main concern is the same marine influence increasing after 5 PM and cloud cover building. Winds from 287–305° are on-site for this launch throughout the flyable window. Gusts not a significant concern at this elevation.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 5–6 kts NW, clearing by 9 AM. Decent for a morning sled-ride or light soaring if wind fills in consistently. Direction 287–312° is workable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 7–9 kts WNW (287–303°), BL 350–360 m. Best soaring conditions of the day — steady ridge cycles, clear sky, good visibility. Solid P2+ conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 8–9 kts WNW holding through 3–4 PM, then gradually easing and backing slightly. Cloud cover increases after 5 PM. Window closes around 5:30–6 PM.

verdict.hazards: Shallow thermal ceiling limits altitude gain; marine layer return after 5 PM; early morning variable winds

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze. It needs sustained W–WNW winds at 10–20 kts for good soaring. Winds from 270–310° are on-site; too far north creates cross/rotor conditions. The marine layer dynamics are critical — deep fog suppresses lift while a cleared, sun-warmed ridge with steady sea breeze is ideal.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is the standout site today. KHAF is reporting 310–340° at 9–11 kts and CLR/FEW015 — the marine layer at the coast has largely cleared, and the sea breeze is well established. The NDBC 46012 buoy at Half Moon Bay shows SST 13.8°C with 0.7 m waves at 12-second period, indicating a moderate swell but relatively benign sea state; the cool SST supports a persistent sea breeze but not an overwhelming marine layer. Surface winds at Mussel Rock in the models build from 6.5 kts at 8 AM to 10–12 kts by afternoon from 288–300° — textbook on-site direction. At 80 m, winds reach 11–14 kts. The one watch item is the cloud cover oscillating between 22–100% through the day (patchy marine stratus trying to reform), which will create variable thermal boost and occasional shadow cycles. GFS and HRRR are in full agreement on this site for today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 6–7 kts WNW, 100% overcast through 10 AM clearing to 49% by 11 AM. Ridgeable but lift will be weaker under cloud; manageable for experienced pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 7–10 kts W–WNW (288–296°), clouds patchy (50–94%). Best soaring conditions as wind steadies in the ideal direction. Strong ridge effect with 11–13 kts at ridge-top level.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10–12 kts W–WNW, strongest window 3–5 PM at 10.9–11.6 kts. Wind at 80 m hitting 13–14 kts — getting punchy for P2s. Cloud cover increases again 5–6 PM as marine stratus reasserts.

verdict.hazards: Oscillating marine stratus creates variable lift cycles; upper-level winds 13–14 kts by mid-afternoon may be strong/gusty for P2s; cloud reforming after 5 PM

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works as both a ridge and thermal site with W–WNW winds at 8–18 kts. The peak elevation (~790 m MSL) means pilots need the BL to develop well above launch for meaningful thermal flights. Sea breeze arrival from the NW typically kicks in by 11 AM–1 PM and powers afternoon soaring. Wind from 270–320° is on-site.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same fundamental constraint as Ed Levin upper: the KOAK inversion caps the BL at roughly 325–350 m, which translates to a very low ceiling above this higher launch. Morning is completely calm and overcast — near-zero winds through 11 AM make early flight impossible. The midday sea breeze builds to 4–7 kts WNW, reaching 7–8 kts by 2–3 PM, which is adequate but only borderline for reliable ridge cycles. The critical issue is that at 120 m (near launch elevation equivalent), model winds show 8–11 kts by afternoon — adequate directional flow exists. KRHV confirms warming to 23°C clearing by afternoon, which will help thermal initiation, but the low BL cap limits usable altitude to a narrow band. The afternoon window exists but is narrow and requires patience for cycles.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near-calm (0.4–2.8 kts), 100% overcast at 8 AM clearing by 9 AM. Not launchable — insufficient wind for ridge support or thermal development.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds picking up to 4–7 kts WNW (287–296°), BL reaching 325–350 m. Shallow thermals developing. Marginal soaring possible for patient P3 pilots — expect low, scratchy flying.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 7–8 kts WNW by 2–3 PM, best window of the day. 120 m winds at 7–11 kts confirm on-site flow at launch elevation. Cloud cover building again after 5 PM limits the window.

verdict.hazards: Very low thermal ceiling (~350 m AGL), dead calm morning requiring patience, marine influence returning by evening, narrow flyable window

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Wednesday Apr 8 looks stronger: winds build to 10 kts WNW by noon and 10.2 kts by 2 PM (GFS/HRRR agree), BL expanding to 385–390 m. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 13–15 kts at the launch elevation — more energetic but potentially punchy. CAPE of 40 at 2 PM is a minor flag. Better overall but upper launch will be lively; P3+ day.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Strong NW at 9–10 kts by noon, peaking at 10.3 kts at 2 PM. Tomorrow.io gusts 8–13 kts. BL reaches 410 m — best Ed Levin 600 ft day of the forecast window. CAPE of 60 at 2 PM is minor. Good P2+ conditions mid-morning through mid-afternoon with steady WNW flow.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Apr 8 shows a significant change: winds shift from WNW to W–WSW (261–281°), slightly more southerly than ideal. Cloud cover hits 100% from noon onward per models — persistent marine overcast likely. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 18 kts in the morning. Likely overcast and cross-trending by afternoon; MARGINAL to NO FLY depending on cloud burn-off.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to Ed Levin upper: winds build to 10 kts WNW by 2 PM, BL ~385 m. Better than today but still capped. CAPE at 40 kJ/kg is a minor instability flag. P3+ day with a good 11 AM–4 PM window if thermals cycle properly.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Apr 9 raises red flags: 100% cloud cover all day, CAPE reaching 220 kJ/kg by 2 PM with precip probability at 15–19%. BL briefly spikes to 1800 m at 2 PM suggesting convective instability — this is a storm/unstable air pattern. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same story as the upper launch on Apr 9: socked in all day, convective CAPE 200+ kJ/kg, precip likely. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Apr 9: winds shift to SSW (218–230°), directly cross/offshore for the ridge. 100% overcast, significant CAPE (60–120 kJ/kg), precip probability 6–14%. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Apr 9 is a significant weather day: CAPE spikes to 200–310 kJ/kg by 2–4 PM with 100% clouds and 14% precip probability. BL briefly at 1530 m before collapsing — convective thunderstorm potential. Absolute NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No GFS data beyond Apr 9 in this dataset. Apr 9 weather system passage may clear by Apr 10 — monitor next morning brief for updated conditions post-system.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as upper launch — post-system recovery timeline unknown without Apr 10 data. Likely improving but monitor.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Post-system coastal conditions on Apr 10 often reset to good W flow as high pressure rebuilds. Likely improving but no data to confirm — check next brief.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Post-convective event on Apr 9 may leave residual instability or clearing on Apr 10. No data available — monitor next brief closely.

watchlist: Marine stratus at Mussel Rock oscillating 50–100% cloud cover through the day — lift quality will vary significantly with shade cycles
Shallow BL cap (~350 m AGL) from strong 850 mb inversion limits all inland sites to scratchy, low-ceiling flying today
Apr 9 convective event developing: CAPE 200–310 kJ/kg with precip at all sites — plan accordingly and avoid flying Apr 9
Tomorrow.io showing gusts to 18 kts at Mussel Rock on Apr 8 morning — assess carefully before launch
KRHV showing FEW070 which is an unusual cloud deck for inland valley — confirm clearing before heading to Ed Levin
tldr: Today's best bet is Mussel Rock: clean W–WNW sea breeze at 10–12 kts with the marine layer largely burned off, good for an 11 AM–4 PM session (P3 recommended for the punchier afternoon). Ed Levin 600 ft is a solid second choice with a steady 7–9 kt NW breeze and a nice midday window. Skip Apr 9 entirely — convective instability and 100% overcast make it a no-fly day at all sites.
